quotes.verbatim_quote:
I think of articulations as inflections. When someone is speaking not all the words have the same
     accent, volume, inflection etc. When playing lines you need to alter the inflection of the
     notes; not all the notes are long or short or accented.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the book's core conceptual framing of articulation as note-level inflection analogous to speech.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
These inflections are accomplished
     by slurring notes. Pick one note and play two notes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the operational rule that produces articulation: one pick stroke yields two sounded notes via slur.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The following exercises illustrate this point . They are all typical be-bop phrases
quotes.why_load_bearing: Anchors the articulation study in the bebop vocabulary the method is teaching to pick against.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Here is the phrase with the slur moved to the next beat.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes that shifting where the slur falls within an identical phrase changes its inflection, a rehearsal-design principle.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
make sure you play these last two notes,
                                                             long, short.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Concrete articulation rule (long-short) applied to phrase endings, a recurring bebop cadential gesture.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Try moving the slur over any two notes that are on the same string.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the physical constraint governing where slurs are playable on guitar: hammer/pull requires a shared string.

quotes.verbatim_quote: I've added a few slurs. Experiment adding your own slurs at different places
quotes.why_load_bearing: Codifies the method's pedagogical stance that slur placement is a creative choice the student must internalize, not a fixed score.
